prompts: A burndown graph on day 3 shows the team is 'at risk for delivery.' What does this indicate?
If the burndown chart indicates 'at risk for delivery' on day 3 of an iteration, how should the team interpret that signal?

correct.text: It means the team is not meeting their planned progress and may fail to complete the iteration on time.
correct.why: An 'at risk for delivery' signal on a burndown chart indicates actual progress is lagging the planned burn rate, creating schedule risk for the iteration.

distractors.text: It suggests that the team is on track to meet their delivery goals.
distractors.trap: Ignores the warning nature of the burndown signal and misreads risk as healthy progress.

distractors.text: It indicates that the team is exceeding their planned progress and will finish early.
distractors.trap: Mistakenly interprets a negative burndown variance as accelerated performance.

distractors.text: It shows that the project is over budget.
distractors.trap: Confuses an iteration progress indicator with a cost management metric.

distractors.text: It means the iteration scope has already been accepted and only documentation remains.
distractors.trap: Assumes a delivery risk indicator is a sign of near-complete acceptance rather than lagging work completion.

explanation: A burndown chart compares remaining work against the expected rate of completion. When it signals 'at risk for delivery,' the team is burning down work too slowly relative to plan and may miss the iteration goal or committed completion date. The chart is about progress toward finishing planned work, not direct budget status.
references: Agile Practice Guide, section on information radiators and burndown charts
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, section on measurement, monitoring, and adaptive delivery performance indicators

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: In project cost estimating, which method is generally the most accurate and therefore the most commonly used in real-world practice when sufficient detail is available?
Which project estimating technique is typically considered the most accurate and most widely used in practice once detailed component estimates can be developed?

correct.text: Bottom-up estimating
correct.why: Bottom-up estimating is generally the most accurate because it builds the estimate from detailed estimates of lower-level work components.

distractors.text: Top-down estimating
distractors.trap: Confuses a faster, higher-level estimating approach with the most accurate detailed estimating method.

distractors.text: Analogous estimating
distractors.trap: Assumes historical comparison is more accurate than aggregating detailed work package estimates.

distractors.text: Parametric estimating
distractors.trap: Mistakes a statistical estimating technique for the method that is usually most accurate across detailed project work.

distractors.text: Guesstimating
distractors.trap: Represents the misconception that informal judgment without structured inputs is an accepted PMI estimating method.

explanation: Bottom-up estimating is performed by estimating individual work packages or activities and then rolling them up to higher levels. Because it relies on the greatest level of detail, it is typically more accurate than top-down or analogous approaches, though it usually takes more time and effort. PMI describes it as a method used when project scope and component detail are sufficiently defined.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Cost Management, Estimate Costs
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Schedule Management, Estimate Activity Durations
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Planning Process Group

prompts: In project risk management, what term describes the period of time before a risk may affect one or more project objectives?
Which risk attribute refers to how soon a risk could have an impact on project objectives?

correct.text: Proximity
correct.why: Proximity describes the time remaining before a risk may occur or have an impact on project objectives.

distractors.text: Urgency
distractors.trap: Confuses how soon a response may be needed with the formal term for the time before impact.

distractors.text: Detectability
distractors.trap: Mistakes ease of identifying a risk or issue for the timing of its possible effect.

distractors.text: Controllability
distractors.trap: Confuses the degree to which a risk can be influenced with when it may occur.

distractors.text: Propinquity
distractors.trap: Uses a look-alike non-PMI term that sounds similar to the correct concept.

explanation: Proximity is a risk characteristic used to describe how near in time a risk event is to the present or to a point of impact. It helps teams prioritize risks that may affect objectives sooner rather than later. Urgency is related but refers more broadly to how quickly a response is required, not specifically the period before impact.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Risk Management, Perform Qualitative Risk Analysis
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Uncertainty Performance Domain
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Planning Process Group

prompts: Les and his colleagues are working on a large construction project. The environmental, architectural, and marketing specialists are in three different geographic locations. Team members do not have time to travel to a central location for every meeting, but they still want to see one another's nonverbal cues. Which type of virtual meeting is most appropriate?

A geographically distributed project team needs to meet frequently without travel and wants visibility into facial expressions and body language during discussions. Which virtual meeting format should the project manager recommend?

correct.text: Videoconference
correct.why: Videoconferencing enables remote participants to communicate in real time while also observing nonverbal communication.

distractors.text: Teleconference
distractors.trap: Assumes audio-only communication is sufficient even when visual nonverbal cues are explicitly needed.

distractors.text: Chat room
distractors.trap: Confuses fast text communication with a medium that supports tone, facial expression, and body language.

distractors.text: Listserv
distractors.trap: Mistakes an asynchronous email distribution method for a live interactive meeting format.

distractors.text: Discussion forum
distractors.trap: Represents the misconception that any online collaboration tool can substitute for synchronous visual meetings.

explanation: The deciding requirement is the need to observe nonverbal communication without travel. Videoconferencing supports synchronous discussion plus visual cues, making it more effective than teleconferencing, chat, or email-based tools for this scenario. PMI recognizes rich communication channels as especially valuable when nuance and alignment matter.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Communications Management, Manage Communications
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Resource Management, Develop Team
Agile Practice Guide, Section on Communication and Colocation Alternatives

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: Which of the following is an output of the 'Determine Budget' process in project management?
In PMI process terminology, what is a valid output of the 'Determine Budget' process?

correct.text: Cost baseline
correct.why: The cost baseline is a formal output of the 'Determine Budget' process and is used to measure and monitor project cost performance.

distractors.text: Stakeholder register
distractors.trap: Confuses a stakeholder management artifact with a cost management process output.

distractors.text: Project charter
distractors.trap: Mistakes an initiating document for an output produced during budget determination.

distractors.text: Work breakdown structure
distractors.trap: Confuses a scope decomposition artifact used as an input with a budget process output.

distractors.text: Quality management plan
distractors.trap: Assumes any planning document may be produced by cost budgeting rather than its specific knowledge area process.

explanation: The original source mixed several process names, inputs, and outputs together, but the PMI-correct output from 'Determine Budget' is the cost baseline, along with project funding requirements and updates to project documents. The other options listed are created in different processes or used as inputs rather than outputs. This keeps the concept tested aligned to the 'Determine Budget' process and preserves a single correct answer.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Cost Management, Determine Budget
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Planning Process Group

prompts: Mark is the project manager for a new software development project. He takes a hands-off approach, allowing the team to make many of its own decisions and set its own goals. Which leadership style is Mark using?
A project manager gives the team broad autonomy, intervenes minimally, and allows members to determine much of their own direction. What leadership style does this describe?

correct.text: Laissez-faire
correct.why: Laissez-faire leadership is a hands-off style in which the leader allows substantial team autonomy and limited direct intervention.

distractors.text: Transactional
distractors.trap: Confuses reward-and-compliance management with a largely noninterventionist leadership approach.

distractors.text: Transformational
distractors.trap: Mistakes inspirational change-oriented leadership for a deliberately hands-off style.

distractors.text: Charismatic
distractors.trap: Assumes strong personal influence and inspiration are the same as low-directive autonomy.

distractors.text: Servant leadership
distractors.trap: Confuses empowering and supporting the team with withdrawing direction almost entirely.

explanation: Laissez-faire leadership is characterized by minimal direct supervision and broad freedom for the team to act independently. That distinguishes it from transformational, transactional, or charismatic styles, which all involve more active leadership behavior. In PMI terms, understanding leadership style helps a project manager choose the approach best suited to team maturity and context.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Resource Management, Manage Team
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Team Performance Domain
Agile Practice Guide, Section on Servant Leadership and Team Empowerment

prompts: Organizational process assets influence how projects are managed. Which option best describes the main categories of organizational process assets?
A project manager is reviewing organizational process assets before planning work. Which choice best identifies the important categories of organizational process assets?

correct.text: Organization knowledge bases, processes, policies, and procedures
correct.why: This matches the recognized categories of organizational process assets, which include an organization's knowledge repositories and its established processes, policies, and procedures.

distractors.text: Organization knowledge bases, tools, and techniques
distractors.trap: Confuses organizational assets with project management tools and techniques used by practitioners.

distractors.text: Processes, policies, procedures, tools, and techniques
distractors.trap: Incorrectly treats tools and techniques as categories of organizational process assets while omitting organizational knowledge bases.

distractors.text: Organization knowledge bases and processes
distractors.trap: Assumes a partial list is sufficient and overlooks policies and procedures as distinct organizational assets.

distractors.text: Enterprise environmental factors, policies, procedures, and lessons learned
distractors.trap: Mixes organizational process assets with enterprise environmental factors, which are a different concept.

explanation: Organizational process assets are internal organizational resources that can influence project success. They include repositories of knowledge such as lessons learned, along with formal processes, policies, and procedures. Tools and techniques are methods applied by the project team, not categories of organizational process assets.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Models, Methods, and Artifacts section on organizational process assets
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, section 2.3 Enterprise Environmental Factors and Organizational Process Assets

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: Parametric estimating is best described as which of the following?
A project manager needs to estimate cost or duration by applying a measurable rate from historical data. Which statement best describes parametric estimating?

correct.text: Using a statistical relationship to a past project such as cost per unit to calculate the cost or duration
correct.why: Parametric estimating uses historical data and a statistical relationship, such as hours per drawing or cost per unit, to forecast cost or duration.

distractors.text: Using a previous project to calculate the cost or duration, making adjustments for known differences
distractors.trap: Confuses parametric estimating with analogous estimating, which relies on comparison to a prior project.

distractors.text: Breaking an activity down into smaller components that are easier to estimate, then aggregating the cost or durations
distractors.trap: Represents bottom-up estimating rather than applying a statistical rate.

distractors.text: A weighted average method of estimating costs or durations
distractors.trap: Represents three-point or PERT-style estimating, not parametric estimating.

distractors.text: Adding expert judgments from multiple stakeholders to create a consensus estimate
distractors.trap: Mistakes expert judgment or consensus techniques for a statistical estimating method.

explanation: Parametric estimating depends on a quantified relationship between variables and historical data. A common example is multiplying units by a known rate such as cost per meter or hours per component. This differs from analogous, bottom-up, and three-point estimating because those use different estimation logic.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, section 6.4.2.2 Parametric Estimating
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, section 7.2.2.2 Parametric Estimating

eco_task: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
topic: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
prompts: Portfolio management involves which of the following?
An organization uses portfolio management to align investment decisions with strategy. Which statement best describes portfolio management?

correct.text: Managing various projects or programs to achieve strategic business objectives
correct.why: Portfolio management coordinates projects, programs, and other work to align investments and outcomes with strategic business goals.

distractors.text: Managing several related projects
distractors.trap: Describes program management, which focuses on related projects rather than the broader strategic portfolio.

distractors.text: Managing one high-priority project
distractors.trap: Confuses portfolio management with individual project management regardless of priority.

distractors.text: Managing all the projects of one team
distractors.trap: Assumes portfolio scope is defined by a team boundary instead of strategic alignment.

distractors.text: Coordinating project schedules to reduce resource conflicts within one department
distractors.trap: Reduces portfolio management to resource coordination rather than strategic selection and oversight.

explanation: Portfolio management is strategy driven and focuses on selecting and overseeing components to achieve organizational objectives. It can include projects, programs, subportfolios, and operations as appropriate. Program management, by contrast, is centered on coordinated management of related projects to realize benefits not available if managed separately.
references: The Standard for Portfolio Management Fourth Edition, section 1 Introduction and strategic alignment concepts
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, section 1.2.3.4 Portfolio Management

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
prompts: Project management processes are best described as which of the following?
Which statement correctly defines project management processes in a PMI context?

correct.text: A systematic series of activities directed toward causing an end result in which one or more inputs are acted upon to create one or more outputs.
correct.why: This matches PMI's definition of a process as a set of interrelated actions and activities performed to create a pre-specified product, result, or service.

distractors.text: The series of phases that a project passes through from its start to its completion.
distractors.trap: Confuses project management processes with the project life cycle.

distractors.text: A logical grouping of project management inputs, tools and techniques, and outputs.
distractors.trap: Mistakes a process description framework for the definition of a process itself.

distractors.text: A collection of logically related project activities that culminates in the completion of one or more deliverables.
distractors.trap: Confuses a process with a project phase or work package that produces deliverables.

distractors.text: A formal list of project management documents used to control and report project performance.
distractors.trap: Assumes processes are artifacts rather than actions and activities.

explanation: A project management process is an action-oriented concept, not a phase, artifact collection, or deliverable grouping. PMI defines a process as interrelated actions and activities that transform inputs into outputs. The distractors reflect common confusion among process, life cycle, and documentation concepts.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), 6th Edition, Section 1.2.3
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), 7th Edition, Process-related concepts and models

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
prompts: Project management tools and techniques assist project managers and their teams in which of the following?
How do project management tools and techniques support the work of the project manager and team?

correct.text: All of the above
correct.why: Project management tools and techniques support work across knowledge areas, including planning and controlling resources, schedule, cost, quality, procurement, and project information.

distractors.text: Carrying out human resource, integration, and procurement management
distractors.trap: Treats tools and techniques as limited to selected knowledge areas rather than broadly applicable across the project.

distractors.text: Carrying out scope, time, cost, and quality management
distractors.trap: Assumes tools and techniques apply only to the traditional planning and control constraints.

distractors.text: Generating, collecting, disseminating, and storing project information
distractors.trap: Reduces tools and techniques to communications and information management only.

distractors.text: Only monitoring project progress after the project management plan has been approved
distractors.trap: Incorrectly assumes tools and techniques are used only in monitoring and controlling, not throughout the project.

explanation: PMI tools and techniques are used throughout the project to support work across multiple domains and knowledge areas. Because each listed area is a valid use of tools and techniques, the inclusive option is correct. The distractors each reflect an overly narrow view of their purpose.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), 6th Edition, Section 1.2.4
Process Groups: A Practice Guide, Process concepts and application of tools and techniques

prompts: Project Risk Management is best defined as what?
Which statement correctly describes Project Risk Management?

correct.text: Focuses on identifying and responding appropriately to risks that can impact the project.
correct.why: Project Risk Management is concerned with planning for risk, identifying risks, analyzing them, and responding in ways that improve project outcomes.

distractors.text: Focuses on planning, developing, and managing the quality environment so the project can meet or exceed stakeholder expectations.
distractors.trap: Confuses risk management with quality management.

distractors.text: Includes identifying the project phases and activities and estimating, sequencing, and assigning resources.
distractors.trap: Confuses risk management with schedule and resource planning.

distractors.text: Focuses on coordinating development of the project plan, executing the work, and controlling changes.
distractors.trap: Confuses risk management with project integration management.

distractors.text: Ensures all stakeholders receive timely project status updates and stored project records.
distractors.trap: Mistakes communications management for risk management.

explanation: Project Risk Management is specifically about uncertainty that may affect project objectives and the planned responses to that uncertainty. It is distinct from quality, schedule, integration, and communications management, even though those areas interact with risk. The distractors each map to a different knowledge area, which is the intended misconception.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), 6th Edition, Section 11
Process Groups: A Practice Guide, Risk Management processes

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
prompts: Scaled Agile Framework, or SAFe, organizes the enterprise around value streams and defines practices, roles, and activities at portfolio, program, and team levels. Which option is NOT one of the SAFe principles?
In SAFe, which of the following is not a recognized Lean-Agile principle?

correct.text: Make sure stakeholders know when you did more work than your teammates.
correct.why: This is not a SAFe principle and conflicts with SAFe's emphasis on collaboration, systems thinking, and customer value.

distractors.text: Apply cadence and synchronize with cross-domain planning.
distractors.trap: May look unfamiliar, but it is an actual SAFe principle about alignment across teams and functions.

distractors.text: Unlock the intrinsic motivation of knowledge workers.
distractors.trap: Seems soft or people-focused, leading some to wrongly exclude it even though it is a SAFe principle.

distractors.text: Decentralize decision making.
distractors.trap: Can appear too broad to be formal guidance, but it is a core SAFe principle.

distractors.text: Take an economic view.
distractors.trap: May be overlooked because it sounds strategic rather than agile, but it is a foundational SAFe principle.

explanation: SAFe principles center on flow, value delivery, decentralized decisions, alignment, and motivation of knowledge workers. The correct option is intentionally individualistic and competitive in a way that runs against Lean-Agile thinking. The other options are all legitimate SAFe principles, even if some sound less familiar than others.
references: Agile Practice Guide, Section 3, Agile, Lean, and Hybrid Mindset
PMIstandards+ Agile and scaling agile references aligned to enterprise agile practices

prompts: Schedule Variance is defined as which formula?
In earned value management, what is the correct formula for Schedule Variance, or SV?

correct.text: BCWP - BCWS
correct.why: In traditional earned value terminology, Schedule Variance equals earned value minus planned value, expressed as BCWP minus BCWS.

distractors.text: ACWP - BCWP
distractors.trap: Confuses schedule variance with a cost-based comparison using actual cost and earned value.

distractors.text: BCWP - ACWP
distractors.trap: Confuses schedule variance with cost variance, which compares earned value to actual cost.

distractors.text: BCWS - BCWP
distractors.trap: Reverses the correct schedule variance formula and would invert the interpretation.

distractors.text: BAC - ACWP
distractors.trap: Confuses schedule variance with a remaining budget or estimate concept.

explanation: Schedule Variance measures whether the project is ahead of or behind the approved schedule baseline in earned value terms. Using older PMI terminology, BCWP represents earned value and BCWS represents planned value, so SV equals BCWP minus BCWS. Several distractors deliberately substitute cost formulas or reverse the subtraction order.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), 6th Edition, Section 7.4.2.2
Process Groups: A Practice Guide, Earned Value Analysis

prompts: Since the start of an agile project, the Product Owner repeatedly uses iteration ceremonies to ask about the budget spent for each increment and seems more focused on cost than on the product. What should the Project Manager have done earlier to change this behavior?

In an agile project, the Product Owner keeps turning sprint ceremonies into discussions about money spent per iteration instead of product outcomes. What should the Project Manager have addressed earlier to prevent this?

correct.text: Worked with the Product Owner to clarify their role in an Agile project and the scope and purpose of the Agile ceremonies
correct.why: Clarifying the Product Owner's role and the intended purpose of agile events would redirect attention toward product value and backlog outcomes rather than detailed cost tracking during ceremonies.

distractors.text: Used different estimating approaches so the Product Owner would feel more confident about the cost spent in each increment
distractors.trap: Assumes the issue is estimation accuracy when the real problem is misunderstanding of role boundaries and ceremony objectives.

distractors.text: Negotiated a fixed-price contract so the Product Owner would focus on value instead of money
distractors.trap: Confuses a contracting mechanism with stakeholder behavior coaching and agile role clarity.

distractors.text: Created a communications management plan with reports comparing actual and planned spending for each iteration so budget questions would not arise in ceremonies
distractors.trap: Treats symptoms through reporting instead of correcting the Product Owner's misunderstanding of how agile ceremonies should be used.

distractors.text: Escalated the Product Owner's behavior to the sponsor because financial concerns should not be discussed in agile projects
distractors.trap: Mistakenly treats cost concern as inappropriate altogether instead of recognizing that the issue is the time and forum in which it is raised.

explanation: The issue is not that the Product Owner cares about cost, but that they are using agile ceremonies for the wrong purpose. The better early intervention is to align on responsibilities, expected behaviors, and the goal of each ceremony. In agile, ceremonies are intended to inspect and adapt product work, while financial reporting can be handled through other channels.
references: Agile Practice Guide, section on Agile Roles
Agile Practice Guide, section on Agile Events and Ceremonies
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, section on Tailoring and Team

prompts: Situational leadership would suggest that which statement is true?
According to situational leadership, what determines effective leadership behavior?

correct.text: Behavior of effective leaders depends upon the situation
correct.why: Situational leadership holds that leaders adapt their style to the needs of the people and circumstances rather than relying on a single fixed approach.

distractors.text: Theory Y beliefs about human behavior always lead to success as a situational leader
distractors.trap: Confuses a motivation theory with the broader principle of adapting leadership style to context.

distractors.text: One leadership style is appropriate in all work situations
distractors.trap: Represents the opposite of situational leadership by assuming a universal best style.

distractors.text: Leaders should rely primarily on referent power to be successful
distractors.trap: Mistakes a source of power for a complete leadership model.

distractors.text: Effective leaders should avoid changing their approach because consistency matters more than context
distractors.trap: Assumes adaptability is a weakness rather than a core principle of situational leadership.

explanation: Situational leadership is based on the idea that no single style works best in every circumstance. Effective leaders assess the context, including team capability and commitment, and then adjust their behavior accordingly. The other choices confuse leadership style with power, motivation theory, or rigid consistency.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, section 9.5 Manage Team
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, section 4.1 Interpersonal and Team Skills
Process Groups Practice Guide, section on Leadership

eco_task: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
topic: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
prompts: Stakeholders are individuals or organizations actively involved in the project or whose interests may be positively or negatively affected by its performance or completion. What is the best reason to perform stakeholder analysis during stakeholder identification?
Why is stakeholder analysis important in the Identify Stakeholders process?

correct.text: Stakeholder satisfaction is a key success criterion in a project
correct.why: Stakeholder analysis is essential because project success depends heavily on understanding stakeholder interests, influence, and expectations so they can be engaged appropriately.

distractors.text: Stakeholders are needed primarily to build the WBS and scope statement
distractors.trap: Reduces stakeholder analysis to a scope-definition activity instead of recognizing its broader role in project success and engagement.

distractors.text: Stakeholders are analyzed mainly because they help conduct procurements
distractors.trap: Incorrectly narrows stakeholder importance to procurement support.

distractors.text: Stakeholder analysis is performed so only supportive stakeholders are included in planning
distractors.trap: Assumes resistant or neutral stakeholders can be ignored, which undermines effective engagement.

distractors.text: The main purpose of stakeholder analysis is to delegate project decisions away from the project manager
distractors.trap: Misunderstands stakeholder analysis as a governance transfer rather than an input to communication and engagement planning.

explanation: Projects are judged not only by delivery metrics but also by how well stakeholder needs and expectations are addressed. Stakeholder analysis helps identify influence, interest, impact, and engagement strategies early. That makes stakeholder satisfaction a central reason for performing the analysis.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, section 13.1 Identify Stakeholders
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, section 13.2 Plan Stakeholder Engagement
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Stakeholders Performance Domain

prompts: Stuart Sutcliffe has just formed a five-person project team to implement a new learning management system at Stonehill. None of the members know each other, and three are brand new to Stonehill, including one who just moved to the area. According to Maslow's hierarchy of needs, which need should Stuart be most concerned with first in order to motivate the team?

A newly formed project team includes several people who are new to both the organization and one another. Based on Maslow's hierarchy, which need should the project manager focus on first to motivate the team?

correct.text: Social
correct.why: Because the team is newly formed and members do not know one another, belonging and connection are the most immediate motivational needs to address.

distractors.text: Safety
distractors.trap: Assumes physical or job security concerns are the primary issue when the scenario points more directly to belonging and team connection.

distractors.text: Physiological
distractors.trap: Misapplies the lowest level of Maslow's hierarchy to a workplace scenario where basic survival needs are not the central concern.

distractors.text: Esteem
distractors.trap: Jumps to recognition and status needs before the team has established basic social bonds.

distractors.text: Self-actualization
distractors.trap: Assumes peak fulfillment is the first motivator even though more foundational interpersonal needs remain unmet.

explanation: The scenario highlights that team members are unfamiliar with one another and with the organization, which points to a need for belonging and interpersonal connection. In Maslow's model, social needs become important once basic needs are reasonably met. For a newly formed team, helping people feel included and connected supports motivation and collaboration.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, section 9.4 Develop Team
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, section 4.1 Interpersonal and Team Skills
Process Groups Practice Guide, section on Team Development and Motivation

prompts: A project manager is reviewing classic motivation theories with the team. Which theory includes the assumptions that work is natural, people do not need to be coerced to perform, and individuals will commit to objectives when achieving them leads to rewards?
Which management theory assumes that work is a natural activity, people can self-direct without coercion, and individuals will commit to goals when achievement is linked to rewards?

correct.text: MacGregor's 'Theory Y'
correct.why: Theory Y assumes people are generally self-motivated, view work as natural, and will commit to objectives when conditions support achievement and rewards.

distractors.text: the work of Mooney and Reiley
distractors.trap: Confuses general management theorists with a specific motivation theory about assumptions of human behavior at work.

distractors.text: Taylor's 'scientific management' theory
distractors.trap: Mistakenly equates efficiency and task optimization with a people-centered theory of intrinsic motivation and self-direction.

distractors.text: MacGregor's 'Theory X'
distractors.trap: Reverses the concept by choosing the theory that assumes people dislike work and require control or coercion.

distractors.text: Maslow's hierarchy of needs
distractors.trap: Confuses a needs-based motivation framework with McGregor's managerial assumptions about worker behavior.

explanation: The stem describes a positive view of human motivation and responsibility that aligns with Theory Y. In PMI leadership and team management contexts, this concept supports empowerment, commitment, and participative management. Theory X is the opposite assumption set, while the other options are different management concepts rather than the specific theory described.
references: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Team Performance Domain
PMI Process Groups Practice Guide, Team and Leadership Concepts

prompts: What is the best definition of project risk management?
Which option best defines risk management in a project environment?

correct.text: The process of identifying, analyzing, and responding to risk
correct.why: This captures the core risk management activities recognized by PMI, covering risk identification, analysis, and planned response.

distractors.text: The process of reducing risk to the minimum level possible for the project
distractors.trap: Assumes all risk should be minimized rather than managed appropriately, including accepting or exploiting some risks.

distractors.text: Creation of the risk response plan
distractors.trap: Treats one output or component of risk management as if it were the full discipline.

distractors.text: The process of proactively ensuring that all project risk is documented and controlled
distractors.trap: Overemphasizes documentation and control while omitting analysis, prioritization, and response strategy.

distractors.text: The process of eliminating all uncertainty before project execution begins
distractors.trap: Reflects the false belief that project risk management can remove all uncertainty from a project.

explanation: Risk management is broader than simply documenting risks or writing a response plan. PMI defines it as a structured process that includes identifying risks, analyzing them, and planning and implementing responses. Effective risk management optimizes outcomes rather than trying to eliminate every possible risk.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 11 Project Risk Management
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Uncertainty Performance Domain

prompts: The CEO of a company with many remote employees wants to hold a weekly meeting in which the head of the R&D department demonstrates new prototypes the team is developing. Which communication tool is best for this situation?
In a company with many remote workers, leadership wants a weekly session where the R&D leader shows and explains new prototypes to attendees. Which tool is the best choice?

correct.text: video conference
correct.why: Video conferencing best supports live verbal discussion plus visual demonstration for distributed participants in a recurring remote meeting.

distractors.text: multi-feature online communication platform
distractors.trap: Chooses a broad category of tools instead of the most direct fit for a live visual demonstration meeting.

distractors.text: web conferencing with screen sharing
distractors.trap: Assumes showing on-screen material is the same as demonstrating physical or visual prototypes to a remote audience.

distractors.text: VOIP call
distractors.trap: Ignores the need for visual interaction and demonstration by focusing only on audio communication.

distractors.text: email distribution list
distractors.trap: Substitutes asynchronous status sharing for an interactive meeting that requires real-time demonstration and discussion.

explanation: The scenario requires both synchronous communication and a strong visual component so remote employees can observe prototypes and interact in real time. Video conferencing is the best fit because it supports richer communication cues than audio alone. More generic or asynchronous tools are less suitable for demonstration-based stakeholder engagement.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 10 Project Communications Management
Agile Practice Guide, Communication and Collaboration for Distributed Teams

eco_task: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/Deliverables
topic: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/Deliverables
prompts: The characteristics of a product that determine whether it meets specified requirements are known as what?
What is the term for the conditions or characteristics used to determine whether a product or deliverable satisfies requirements?

correct.text: acceptance criteria
correct.why: Acceptance criteria define the conditions that a product or deliverable must satisfy to be formally accepted as meeting requirements.

distractors.text: quality criteria
distractors.trap: Confuses general quality characteristics with the specific standards used for formal acceptance of deliverables.

distractors.text: success criteria
distractors.trap: Mixes project-level measures of overall success with product-level conditions for accepting a deliverable.

distractors.text: product criteria
distractors.trap: Uses a plausible but nonstandard label instead of the PMI term tied to validating requirements and deliverables.

distractors.text: scope baseline
distractors.trap: Confuses a planning baseline with the explicit conditions used to judge whether a specific deliverable is acceptable.

explanation: Acceptance criteria are the documented conditions that define whether a deliverable satisfies requirements and can be accepted. PMI uses this concept in scope validation and quality-related work to create a common understanding of done and acceptable results. The distractors sound plausible but are either too broad, at the wrong level, or not the standard term.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 5 Project Scope Management
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Validate Scope
Agile Practice Guide, Definition of Done and Acceptance Criteria

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: Cost performance analysis should identify work packages with a negative cost variance or a cost performance index of less than what value?
During earned value analysis, a project manager wants to flag work packages with negative cost variance or poor cost efficiency. A cost performance index below which threshold should be identified?

correct.why: A cost performance index below 1.0 indicates the work is costing more than planned for the value earned.

distractors.trap: Confuses CPI with a percentage-style scale instead of its actual earned value index interpretation.
Assumes CPI uses an arbitrary rating scale rather than a cost efficiency ratio centered around 1.0.
Mistakenly treats only complete failure as unfavorable, ignoring that any CPI below 1.0 signals over-budget performance.
Misunderstands earned value metrics by assuming a higher whole-number threshold is the benchmark for concern.

explanation: In earned value management, CPI equals EV divided by AC, and 1.0 is the break-even point. A CPI under 1.0 means the project is getting less value for each unit of cost than planned, which signals unfavorable cost performance. That is why work packages below this threshold should be highlighted in cost performance analysis.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 7.4 Control Costs
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Earned Value Analysis

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: The cost performance index, or CPI, measures how efficiently project costs are being managed. If cumulative earned value is greater than cumulative actual cost, what can you conclude about CPI?
On a project using earned value management, the cumulative earned value exceeds the cumulative actual cost. What does this indicate about the cost performance index, CPI?

correct.text: CPI is greater than 1.0
correct.why: Because CPI equals EV divided by AC, a value of EV that is greater than AC produces a CPI above 1.0.

distractors.text: CPI cannot be determined from cumulative earned value and cumulative actual cost
distractors.trap: Assumes CPI requires additional data even though the formula uses only EV and AC.

distractors.text: CPI is negative
distractors.trap: Confuses index behavior with variance behavior and ignores that EV and AC are nonnegative in normal earned value calculations.

distractors.text: CPI equals 1.0
distractors.trap: Represents the misconception that any positive cost performance result means the project is exactly on budget.

distractors.text: CPI is less than 1.0
distractors.trap: Reverses the interpretation of the ratio and treats higher actual cost efficiency as poorer performance.

explanation: CPI is calculated as EV divided by AC. When earned value is greater than actual cost, the project is getting more value than it is spending, so CPI must be above 1.0. This indicates favorable cost efficiency.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Measurement Performance Domain
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Project Cost Management section on Earned Value Analysis

prompts: What is the first step in the risk management process?
When beginning risk management on a project, what should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Recognize the threat or threats
correct.why: Risk management begins by identifying potential threats before they can be analyzed, prioritized, or addressed.

distractors.text: Mitigate the threat
distractors.trap: Assumes response planning should occur before the risk has been formally identified and understood.

distractors.text: Assess the effectiveness of countermeasures
distractors.trap: Jumps to monitoring responses before risks have even been identified or treated.

distractors.text: Apply uniform industry-accepted risk management principles
distractors.trap: Confuses general governance or methodology guidance with the first practical step in managing project risk.

distractors.text: Transfer the threat to a third party
distractors.trap: Mistakes a specific risk response strategy for the starting point of the overall risk management process.

explanation: Before a team can analyze or respond to risks, it must first identify them. Recognizing threats is the foundational step that enables subsequent assessment, prioritization, and response planning. Without identification, the rest of risk management cannot be performed effectively.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Uncertainty Performance Domain
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Project Risk Management section on Identify Risks

eco_task: Plan and Manage Project/Phase Closure or Transitions
topic: Plan and Manage Project/Phase Closure or Transitions
prompts: The five project management process groups are Initiating, Planning, Executing, Monitoring and Controlling, and what final process group?
In PMI process group terminology, which process group completes the set of Initiating, Planning, Executing, Monitoring and Controlling, and one more?

correct.text: Closing
correct.why: Closing is the fifth PMI process group and covers formally completing the project or phase.

distractors.text: Timing
distractors.trap: Treats a knowledge area concept as if it were a formal PMI process group.

distractors.text: Costing
distractors.trap: Confuses cost management subject matter with the standardized set of process groups.

distractors.text: Risking
distractors.trap: Invents a process group by turning a management area into a process group label.

distractors.text: Controlling
distractors.trap: Assumes controlling stands alone even though PMI defines Monitoring and Controlling as one combined process group.

explanation: PMI defines five process groups: Initiating, Planning, Executing, Monitoring and Controlling, and Closing. Closing is the group used to finalize all activities and formally complete the project or phase. The other options are not recognized process groups.
references: Process Groups A Practice Guide, Process Groups Overview
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 1.2.3 Process Groups

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: Which formula correctly calculates cost variance, or CV, in earned value management?
In earned value management, what is the correct expression for cost variance, CV?

correct.text: CV = EV - AC
correct.why: Cost variance measures whether the earned value of work performed is greater or less than the actual cost incurred.

distractors.text: CV = BAC - (AC - EV)
distractors.trap: Introduces budget at completion into a metric that should compare only earned value and actual cost.

distractors.text: CV = AC - EV
distractors.trap: Reverses the formula and would invert the meaning of favorable and unfavorable cost performance.

distractors.text: CV = SV - BC
distractors.trap: Combines unrelated terms and reflects confusion between cost and schedule metrics.

distractors.text: CV = EV - PV
distractors.trap: Uses the schedule variance formula instead of the cost variance formula.

explanation: Cost variance is the difference between the value earned and the actual cost spent to achieve that value. The correct formula is EV minus AC. A positive CV indicates the project is under budget, while a negative CV indicates it is over budget.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Measurement Performance Domain
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Project Cost Management section on Earned Value Analysis

prompts: Which leadership style describes a leader who primarily serves others and emphasizes the personal growth and development of team members?
A leader focuses on serving the team and helping followers grow personally and professionally. What leadership style is this?

correct.text: Servant leadership
correct.why: Servant leadership centers on serving team members and supporting their growth, which matches the scenario exactly.

distractors.text: Transformational leadership
distractors.trap: Confuses inspiring broad organizational change with the servant leader focus on meeting follower needs first.

distractors.text: Authentic leadership
distractors.trap: Mistakes self-awareness and transparency for a leadership model defined by service to others.

distractors.text: Transactional leadership
distractors.trap: Assumes leadership is mainly based on rewards and compliance rather than development and service.

distractors.text: Laissez-faire leadership
distractors.trap: Confuses empowering people with providing little guidance or active support.

explanation: The defining feature in the scenario is that the leader exists to serve others and prioritizes follower growth. That is the hallmark of servant leadership in PMI materials, especially in agile and team-focused contexts. The other choices describe different leadership approaches but do not place service to team members at the center.
references: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Team and Leadership
Agile Practice Guide, Servant Leadership

prompts: Which project management tool shows planned and completed tasks along a timeline?
A project manager wants a visual timeline displaying scheduled work and progress against completed tasks. Which tool should be used?

correct.text: Gantt chart
correct.why: A Gantt chart displays activities against time, making it useful for viewing planned and completed tasks on a timeline.

distractors.text: Interrelationship diagram
distractors.trap: Confuses cause-and-effect relationship analysis with schedule visualization over time.

distractors.text: Tree diagram
distractors.trap: Mistakes hierarchical decomposition of elements for a timeline-based scheduling tool.

distractors.text: Affinity diagram
distractors.trap: Assumes grouping ideas by similarity can also show task timing and progress.

distractors.text: Control chart
distractors.trap: Confuses process stability monitoring with displaying scheduled tasks across dates.

explanation: The question asks for a tool that places tasks on a timeline and distinguishes planned from completed work. A Gantt chart is specifically designed for schedule display and tracking. The other tools serve quality, analysis, or idea-grouping purposes rather than time-phased schedule visualization.
references: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Planning Performance Domain
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Develop Schedule

eco_task: Promote Team Performance Through the Application of Emotional Intelligence
topic: Promote Team Performance Through the Application of Emotional Intelligence
prompts: A project manager on a complex project finds that one team member has missed several targets. What should the project manager do first?
During execution of a complex project, a team member repeatedly misses assigned targets. What is the most appropriate initial action for the project manager?

correct.text: Discuss the issue with the team member and offer any needed assistance.
correct.why: The project manager should first address the issue directly and supportively to understand causes and help the team member improve performance.

distractors.text: Determine if the team member has been assigned to a concurrent project.
distractors.trap: Jumps to a possible cause before first speaking with the team member to understand the situation.

distractors.text: Ask the team member's functional manager to address the issue.
distractors.trap: Escalates too early instead of using direct coaching and emotional intelligence first.

distractors.text: Impose a penalty for each day the team member misses a target.
distractors.trap: Assumes punishment is an effective first response to a performance issue.

distractors.text: Reassign all critical tasks to another team member immediately.
distractors.trap: Focuses on replacing the person before diagnosing the problem or offering support.

explanation: When a team member misses targets, the project manager should begin with a direct, respectful conversation to understand the root cause and provide support. This reflects emotional intelligence and a coaching mindset. Escalation, penalties, or reassignment may be considered later if needed, but they are not the best first action.
references: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Team Performance Domain
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Stakeholders and Team
Agile Practice Guide, Emotional Intelligence and Servant Leadership

prompts: A project sponsor requests technical specifications for a project component, but the sponsor is not authorized to view them and insists on access. What should the project manager do first?
The sponsor demands to see restricted technical specifications for a project-related component even though the sponsor lacks authorization. What is the project manager's first action?

correct.text: Meet with the Project Sponsor as soon as possible to discuss the matter further
correct.why: The first step is to clarify the sponsor's need and discuss the issue directly before taking any action that could violate authorization boundaries.

distractors.text: Provide the specifications to the Project Sponsor with some information removed
distractors.trap: Assumes partial disclosure is acceptable even without confirming authority or governance requirements.

distractors.text: Create a stakeholder engagement plan to help the team formulate a response
distractors.trap: Uses a planning artifact instead of addressing the immediate issue directly with the stakeholder.

distractors.text: Update the issue log to make the project team aware and to develop a strategy
distractors.trap: Prioritizes documentation and team escalation before first clarifying the situation with the sponsor.

distractors.text: Send the specifications only after obtaining verbal approval from a team lead
distractors.trap: Treats informal approval as sufficient for access to restricted technical information.

explanation: The best first action is to meet with the sponsor and understand the concern, context, and need behind the request while maintaining compliance with authorization limits. Immediate disclosure, even partial, could breach governance or confidentiality rules. Documentation and broader response planning may follow, but the first step is direct discussion and clarification.
references: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Project Work and Communication
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Uncertainty and Compliance
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Monitor Communications and Manage Issues

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A project team estimates activity costs by breaking each activity into smaller components to improve accuracy. Which estimating technique are they using?
To create a more accurate cost estimate, the team decomposes activities into smaller work elements and estimates those pieces. What estimating technique is this?

correct.text: Bottom-up estimating
correct.why: Bottom-up estimating improves accuracy by estimating smaller work components and aggregating them into a total estimate.

distractors.text: Three-point estimating
distractors.trap: Confuses estimating by optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic ranges with decomposition into smaller parts.

distractors.text: Analogous estimating
distractors.trap: Mistakes estimation based on historical similarity for detailed estimate building from lower-level components.

distractors.text: Parametric estimating
distractors.trap: Assumes use of statistical relationships or unit rates instead of decomposed activity-level buildup.

distractors.text: Expert judgment
distractors.trap: Uses a general estimation input rather than the specific technique described in the scenario.

explanation: The scenario highlights decomposition of activities into smaller chunks to improve estimate precision. That is the defining characteristic of bottom-up estimating. The other methods rely on ranges, historical comparison, statistical relationships, or expert input rather than detailed aggregation from lower-level work.
references: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Planning Performance Domain
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Estimate Costs

eco_task: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
topic: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
prompts: What is the primary purpose of cost-benefit analysis in a project or business case?
In project selection and justification, why is cost-benefit analysis performed?

correct.text: Determine whether the expected benefits outweigh the costs over the long term of the project
correct.why: Cost-benefit analysis compares anticipated benefits with expected costs to support decisions about whether the investment is worthwhile.

distractors.text: Perform a SWOT analysis
distractors.trap: This confuses an investment appraisal technique with a strategic environmental assessment tool.

distractors.text: Increase the share price of the company
distractors.trap: This assumes the purpose of the analysis is to create a market outcome rather than evaluate project value.

distractors.text: Document an investment decision
distractors.trap: This mistakes the purpose of the analysis for one possible administrative output of the decision process.

distractors.text: Estimate the detailed project schedule
distractors.trap: This confuses business justification with time planning.

explanation: Cost-benefit analysis is used to assess whether a proposed initiative is economically justified by comparing expected benefits to expected costs over time. It supports selection and value-based decision making, not strategic scanning, schedule development, or stock price management. Documentation may result from the analysis, but that is not its primary purpose.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 1.2.6.4 Business Documents
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 4.1 Develop Project Charter
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Initiating Process Group, Develop Project Charter

prompts: Which components are included in the scope baseline?
The scope baseline consists of which approved project scope components?

correct.text: Includes the WBS, WBS dictionary, and project scope statement
correct.why: The scope baseline is made up of the approved project scope statement, WBS, and WBS dictionary.

distractors.text: Is not considered part of the project management plan
distractors.trap: This reflects the misconception that baselines sit outside the project management plan rather than being controlled components of it.

distractors.text: Is developed during the Define Scope process
distractors.trap: This confuses a partial input from Define Scope with the full baseline, which is completed after creating the WBS.

distractors.text: Is the most critical document within the planning process
distractors.trap: This assumes PMI ranks one planning artifact as universally most important.

distractors.text: Includes the requirements traceability matrix and milestone list
distractors.trap: This mixes useful scope-related artifacts with the formal components of the approved scope baseline.

explanation: The scope baseline is a formally approved version of the project scope and includes three specific elements: the project scope statement, WBS, and WBS dictionary. It is part of the project management plan and is used for measuring and controlling scope. Other planning artifacts may support scope management, but they are not themselves the scope baseline.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 5.4 Create WBS
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 5.5 Validate Scope
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Planning Process Group, Create WBS

prompts: What does the scope baseline refer to in a project?
In PMI terms, the scope baseline represents what approved view of project scope?

correct.text: What is currently known and approved by the project sponsor and stakeholders
correct.why: A baseline represents the approved version of scope at a point in time and serves as the reference for control.

distractors.text: The scope value as it was presented in the business case
distractors.trap: This assumes initial justification content remains the formal scope baseline without later planning and approval.

distractors.text: The scope as it was defined in the project charter
distractors.trap: This confuses high-level charter scope with the more detailed approved scope baseline.

distractors.text: The working assumptions of the SME responsible for completing work
distractors.trap: This mistakes expert assumptions for approved project scope.

distractors.text: The informal understanding held by the project team before planning is complete
distractors.trap: This treats shared assumptions as equivalent to formally approved baselines.

explanation: The scope baseline is the approved and controlled reference point for project scope. It reflects what is currently defined and approved, not merely what appeared earlier in the business case or charter, and not informal assumptions held by experts or team members. Its approved status is what makes it a baseline.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 5.4 Create WBS
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 5.6 Control Scope
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Planning Process Group, Create WBS

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
prompts: In Scrum, what is the Scrum Master primarily responsible for?
Pick 1: What is the main responsibility of the Scrum Master on a Scrum team?

correct.text: Ensuring Scrum is understood and enacted
correct.why: The Scrum Master is accountable for helping the team and organization understand Scrum theory and practice and ensuring Scrum is applied effectively.

distractors.text: Coordinating the work of the Development Team
distractors.trap: This reflects a command-and-control view that the Scrum Master directs task assignment rather than serving the team.

distractors.text: Delivering the product on budget and to the agreed schedule
distractors.trap: This confuses Scrum Master accountabilities with traditional project manager ownership of budget and schedule.

distractors.text: Solving all impediments
distractors.trap: This assumes the Scrum Master personally fixes every problem instead of helping the team remove impediments and improve flow.

distractors.text: Approving all technical decisions made by developers
distractors.trap: This mistakes servant leadership for technical authority over the team.

explanation: The Scrum Master is responsible for promoting and supporting Scrum by helping everyone understand Scrum theory, practices, rules, and values. The role is not to direct team tasks, own traditional delivery constraints, or personally resolve every issue. The Scrum Master serves the team and the organization by enabling effective Scrum adoption and continuous improvement.
references: Agile Practice Guide, Section 5 Agile Team Performance
Agile Practice Guide, Appendix X3 Agile and Lean Frameworks
The Scrum Guide, Scrum Master Accountabilities

prompts: A sponsor asks the project manager to ensure sufficient management reserve is available for the project. What should the project manager explain is the primary purpose of management reserve?
Why is management reserve included in a project budget when speaking with the team about risk?

correct.text: To take care of the risks that could not be identified by the project team
correct.why: Management reserve is set aside for unforeseen work or unknown risks that were not identified during risk planning.

distractors.text: To take care of the risks that have been identified, but for which no response has been planned
distractors.trap: This confuses management reserve with funding decisions for known risks that should be handled through contingency planning.

distractors.text: To take care of the risks identified for the project activities
distractors.trap: This mistakes management reserve for contingency reserve used for identified known risks.

distractors.text: To take care of the costs that management may incur to review the project activities
distractors.trap: This treats reserve as an overhead budget for governance reviews rather than risk-related funding.

distractors.text: To absorb normal cost variances from poor estimating
distractors.trap: This assumes reserve exists to cover estimation errors and performance problems rather than unforeseen risk.

explanation: Management reserve is budget held outside the cost baseline for unknown risks or unforeseen work within project scope. It is different from contingency reserve, which addresses identified risks. It is not intended for routine management reviews or to mask poor estimating discipline.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 7.3 Determine Budget
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 11 Project Risk Management
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Planning Process Group, Determine Budget

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: The sum of all budgets established for the work to be performed is known as:
In project cost management, what term refers to the total of all approved budgets for the work that will be performed on the project?

correct.text: Budget at Completion
correct.why: Budget at Completion is the total planned value for the project or component and represents the sum of all authorized budgets for the work.

distractors.text: Estimate at Completion
distractors.trap: Confuses the original approved total budget with the current forecast of total cost at completion.

distractors.text: Aggregated Planned Value
distractors.trap: Assumes a nonstandard earned value term is the formal name for the total approved budget.

distractors.text: Planned Value
distractors.trap: Mistakes the time-phased authorized budget for scheduled work at a point in time for the full project budget.

distractors.text: Cost Baseline
distractors.trap: Confuses the approved time-phased budget baseline with the specific earned value metric that names the total planned budget amount.

explanation: Budget at Completion, or BAC, is the earned value term for the total approved budget assigned to the work to be completed. Planned Value refers to authorized budget for scheduled work at a given time, while Estimate at Completion is a forecast. Cost Baseline is related, but BAC is the precise metric asked for here.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Measurement Performance Domain
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 7 Project Cost Management
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Monitoring and Controlling Process Group

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: The technology market is very competitive and getting its product out to market quickly is one of the critical factors for the success of a technology startup. An agile team launches the product with minimal features and plans to learn from the early adopters' feedback. What agile tool or technique is the team embracing in this situation?
A startup team releases a product with only the essential features so it can reach the market quickly and learn from early adopter feedback. Which agile technique is being used?

correct.text: MVP
correct.why: A minimum viable product is a version with just enough features to deliver value, validate assumptions, and gather feedback from early users.

distractors.text: Early launch
distractors.trap: Confuses a general speed-to-market idea with a specific agile product strategy.

distractors.text: MMP
distractors.trap: Mistakes minimum marketable product for the lean experiment of releasing the smallest viable feature set to learn quickly.

distractors.text: Kano Analysis
distractors.trap: Confuses a prioritization and customer satisfaction analysis technique with a release strategy for testing assumptions in the market.

distractors.text: Prototype
distractors.trap: Assumes any early version shown to users is necessarily the same as a viable product released for real-world use and feedback.

explanation: The scenario describes releasing only essential functionality in order to test demand and gather real user feedback quickly, which is the core purpose of an MVP. An MMP focuses on the minimum feature set that is marketable, while Kano Analysis helps classify features by customer satisfaction impact. The emphasis here is learning fast from actual adopters.
references: Agile Practice Guide, Value Delivery and Product Development
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Delivery Performance Domain
Agile Practice Guide, Agile and Lean Frameworks

prompts: The tendency for the project scope to expand over time, usually by changing requirements, specifications, and priorities, is known as:
What term describes the gradual expansion of project scope over time through uncontrolled changes to requirements, specifications, or priorities?

correct.text: Scope creep
correct.why: Scope creep is the uncontrolled expansion of product or project scope without corresponding adjustments to time, cost, and resources.

distractors.text: Project top-up
distractors.trap: Assumes there is a formal PMI term for informal additions to project work.

distractors.text: Scoping
distractors.trap: Confuses the legitimate activity of defining scope with the uncontrolled growth of scope.

distractors.text: Overtime
distractors.trap: Mistakes a schedule or staffing response for the underlying problem of unmanaged scope expansion.

distractors.text: Gold plating
distractors.trap: Confuses extra features added by the team without approval with the broader concept of scope expansion through changing requirements and priorities.

explanation: Scope creep occurs when the project scope expands in an uncontrolled way, often through incremental requirement changes that are not properly managed. Scoping is a normal planning activity, and overtime is only a possible consequence. Gold plating is related but narrower because it refers to adding extras beyond requirements rather than the general growth of scope from multiple sources.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 5 Project Scope Management
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Planning Performance Domain
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Scope Baseline and Change Control

prompts: The Tuckman Model outlines the four classic stages of team development. They are as follows: Select one.
According to the Tuckman Model, which sequence represents the four classic stages of team development?

correct.text: Forming, Storming, Norming, and Performing
correct.why: The classic Tuckman model identifies these four stages as the standard progression of team development.

distractors.text: Developing, Conflicting, Nurturing, and Succeeding
distractors.trap: Relies on intuitive but nonstandard labels instead of the formal Tuckman sequence.

distractors.text: Forming, Conflicting, Normalizing, and Succeeding
distractors.trap: Shows partial familiarity with the model but substitutes informal terms for the actual stage names.

distractors.text: Developing, Convening, Conflicting, and Integrating
distractors.trap: Confuses general team lifecycle language with the specific terminology of the Tuckman framework.

distractors.text: Forming, Planning, Executing, and Closing
distractors.trap: Confuses team development stages with project management process groups.

explanation: Tuckman's classic model of team development consists of Forming, Storming, Norming, and Performing. These stages describe how a team evolves from initial assembly through conflict and alignment to effective collaboration. The distractors use plausible but incorrect substitutes or mix team development with project lifecycle terms.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Team Performance Domain
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 9 Project Resource Management
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Develop Team

prompts: How many project management process groups are defined in PMI's Process Groups framework?
According to PMI, there are how many project management process groups?

correct.why: PMI defines five project management process groups: Initiating, Planning, Executing, Monitoring and Controlling, and Closing.

distractors.trap: Confuses the process groups with a simplified lifecycle model that omits one PMI group.
Assumes PMI adds a separate group for governance or benefits management.
Mixes up process groups with knowledge areas or other framework categorizations.
Mistakes the number of process groups for a larger set of processes or domains.

explanation: PMI's Process Groups framework organizes project management work into five process groups. This is a foundational PMP concept and should not be confused with knowledge areas, principles, or individual processes. The correct answer remains five regardless of project type or industry.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide, Sixth Edition, Part 1, Section 3 Project Management Processes
Process Groups: A Practice Guide, Section 2 Process Groups

prompts: Which type of motivation is based on tangible rewards, comes from outside the individual, and is typically provided by a supervisor or manager?
A manager motivates team members using bonuses, recognition, or other external rewards. What type of motivation is this?

correct.text: extrinsic motivation
correct.why: Extrinsic motivation comes from external rewards or consequences such as pay, bonuses, recognition, or promotions.

distractors.text: external motivation
distractors.trap: Uses a nonstandard label that sounds similar to the accepted motivational theory term.

distractors.text: intrinsic motivation
distractors.trap: Confuses internal personal satisfaction with motivation driven by outside rewards.

distractors.text: legitimate motivation
distractors.trap: Mixes motivation theory with forms of organizational power such as legitimate power.

distractors.text: competitive motivation
distractors.trap: Assumes motivation type is defined by interpersonal rivalry rather than by the source of the reward.

explanation: Extrinsic motivation is driven by factors external to the individual, including compensation, recognition, and managerial rewards. Intrinsic motivation, by contrast, comes from internal satisfaction or personal meaning. PMI team leadership discussions commonly distinguish these two sources of motivation.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide, Sixth Edition, Section 9.4 Develop Team
PMI Process Groups Practice Guide, Team Performance and Motivation discussions

prompts: Through the project management information system, a project management team updates a monthly dashboard with information on project performance. Which communication method does this describe?
A project team posts a monthly performance dashboard in the PMIS so stakeholders can review it whenever needed. What communication method is being used?

correct.text: Pull communication
correct.why: Pull communication is used when information is placed in a central location such as a dashboard or repository for stakeholders to access as needed.

distractors.text: Informal communication
distractors.trap: Confuses the communication method with the level of formality of the message.

distractors.text: Manual communication
distractors.trap: Mistakes the medium or degree of automation for PMI's communication method categories.

distractors.text: Interactive communication
distractors.trap: Assumes all shared project information is interactive, even when stakeholders are only retrieving posted content.

distractors.text: Push communication
distractors.trap: Confuses making information available in a system with directly sending information to recipients.

explanation: A PMIS dashboard that stakeholders access on their own is a classic example of pull communication. In PMI terminology, push communication sends information directly to recipients, while interactive communication involves multidirectional exchange. The defining feature here is that users retrieve the information when they need it.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide, Sixth Edition, Section 10.1 Plan Communications Management
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide, Sixth Edition, Section 10.2 Manage Communications

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
prompts: Tim is managing an organizational workflow automation project and is struggling to identify detailed requirements. Some team members suggest using an adaptive life cycle. Tim worries that moving away from a predictive life cycle will create major scope creep. Which adaptive technique best helps minimize scope creep?

A project manager on a workflow automation project cannot fully define detailed requirements up front. The team proposes an adaptive life cycle, but the manager fears scope creep. Which technique used in adaptive approaches helps control scope creep most effectively?

correct.text: Time-boxing
correct.why: Time-boxing fixes the duration of an iteration so work must be prioritized within a set time limit, helping prevent uncontrolled expansion of scope.

distractors.text: User stories
distractors.trap: Confuses a requirements expression format with the mechanism that constrains scope growth.

distractors.text: Iterations
distractors.trap: Identifies the general delivery cycle but not the specific control technique that limits scope within it.

distractors.text: User epics
distractors.trap: Mistakes a high-level backlog item for a scope control practice.

distractors.text: Backlog refinement
distractors.trap: Assumes clarifying and reprioritizing work is the same as imposing a firm constraint on how much work fits into a cycle.

explanation: In adaptive approaches, scope is commonly managed by fixing time and cost while allowing scope to be refined and prioritized within those constraints. Time-boxing is the specific technique that limits how much work can be taken on during an iteration, which reduces the risk of scope creep.
